These varied perceptions can be both a blessing and a curse, as they shape how others interact with you, but they can also highlight gaps or misunderstandings. When she didn’t like a guy who was trying to pick her up, she started using sign language. This approach would force a new dynamic and often lead to an end to unwanted social interactions, as it required both immediate communication effort and respect for personal space. It effectively shifted the power and the tone of the encounter by introducing a barrier that was both interesting and often difficult to overcome, and was usually met with surprise and often a retreat.

Transitioning to a completely different domain, take, for example, an astronaut in a space station orbiting Earth. The vast open space is silent and desolate, yet surprisingly not devoid of olfactory experiences. It caught him off guard that space smelled of seared steak. This bizarre and unexpected sensation added a mysterious and surreal twist to his journey, making the unfamiliar and sterile environment feel almost ordinary in a bizarre way. This highlights the unexpected ways the universe surprises us, challenging our senses and perceptions beyond our earthly experiences.

Such perceptual challenges are ever-present in human experiences and can be as simple as the idea of mastering a task. When it comes to physical skills, some might say mastery requires a great deal of strength, precision, and practice. It isn't difficult to do a handstand if you just stand on your hands. So, whether it's mastering the complexities of interpersonal relationships, or learning a new skill like a handstand, our approach and attitudes play a significant role in defining both the process and the success.Astronaut, Dr. James Harris, a 64 year old man floated in the tranquil quiet of a space station.

In the silence of deep space, his thoughts wandered back to the moments on the earth's surface. It wasn't any particular thought that gave him pause. He recalled being out on the planet’s surface for a walk when he met a stranger, Mr. John Brown.

At first he was trying to just be neighborly, talking about how beautiful the sunrise was that morning. But that stranger didn't share his sentiment. His response was a barrage of words.  He just didn't appreciate them.  This was something he was used to, many people liked the sound of their own voice more than the sights of the sunrise.

He didn't like the guy, but didn't want to be rude, so he just kept silent. Then he did something unexpected. When she didn’t like a guy who was trying to pick her up, she started using sign language. Sign language was his primary language mode when he was younger.  He had to learn to communicate with the world using sign because he lost his hearing at a young age.  This approach would force a new dynamic to the one-sided conversation and often end the unwanted social interaction. He usually did this to show he didn't like their presence. Even now, people who didn't know his background would be taken back when he used this approach. Often, this would make the person retreat as it required attention and effort from them if they wanted to continue the conversation.

While talking in person with the person left him feeling drained, walking in the crispness of the morning filled his heart with peace. When he looked in the sky he saw a plane high above him moving to the south.

The plane brought to mind an image of an old friend of his Dr. Martin, and a memory of an image that had always stayed in his mind for all those years. Dr. Martin knew him well, and so he was not surprised when he started speaking using sign language. However, he didn't understand the signs of Dr. James when Dr. Martin saw a handstand.  When he would ask why he did or said it. He responded, "It isn't difficult to do a handstand if you just stand on your hands."  He couldn't understand the point of it, why would he need to do this. He did have a point, Dr. Martin would say, but to me it doesn't make any sense.  He said he had no purpose or goal. Dr. James said it helps me to change what I'm going through. When I'm standing on my head, I can think better, he would say to Dr. Martin.

The hawk didn’t understand why the ground squirrels didn’t want to be his friend.

The hawk didn’t understand why the ground squirrels didn’t want to be his friend.

Sometimes, when the sun was just right, it would remind the hawk of sunsets he saw while looking at the river bed.  He dreamed of leaving his law firm to open a portable dog wash. The hospital visit, the tug-of-war with Grandpa Joe, and the strange sense that there’s something more to life than the daily grind. My biggest joy is roasting almonds while stalking prey.

As you continue to massage your calves, focusing your thoughts away from your soreness, you begin to feel lighter. Grandpa Joe, despite his health, always managed a spirited round of tug-of-war, his eyes twinkling with merriment even as he fought the weariness that clung heavily to his limbs. Each tug-of-war session was more of a lesson in resilience, not of physical endurance or play. The warmth that swells within you from the memory leaves you longing.  No doubt in Grandpa Joes mind that time was fleeting, he knew it should be cherished every moment. The days of the tug of war are the fond moments you wish to treasure, and your biggest joy is roasting almonds while stalking prey.

The smoky aroma of grilled meats and vegetables mingled with the sound of laughter and chattering families. But the ants were there to celebrate the scraps that had fallen, the discarded crumbs and bits of food. You could see that ants enjoyed the barbecue more than the family. The hawk above, circling the open field was more of an observer, soaring and searching for a moment the family could never experience. My biggest joy is roasting almonds while stalking prey.

The world around you whispers of untold stories and untouched wild places. And so, with your thoughts drifting back to the hospital and the tug-of-war games, you find comfort in the simple moments, in the joy of nature's mysteries. My biggest joy is roasting almonds while stalking prey.

It is clear you have tried to use a variety of sources and tones to create a vivid picture on the reader: a day at the hospital, nature scenes, a family barbecue, etc. These are all good. However, the writing does not seem to have a direct relationship with one another. For instance, a narrative should contain plot developments that link together. You cannot "remember" your days at the hospital and also play tug of war in a hospital setting.
Similarly, you're alternating perspective between a "character's" thoughts, to the writer stating "the hawk doesn't understand."

I suggest you work on your narrative perspective which includes:
1. Deciding who is telling the story. This could be first-person ("I", "my", "we"), second-person ("you", note you cannot know the thoughts of a character you are supposed to be speaking to or narrating to) or third person ("he, "she", "they", "it"). First person and third person are better for vivid, descriptive settings.
2. Keep the tone of voice consistent throughout, so if you are speaking in the past tense then stick with the past tense. If you're speaking in first person, it is more difficult to jump between your viewpoint and another character's viewpoint.
3. Lastly: Develop each paragraph one by one without the constraint of your sentence as if each paragraph is a part of a narrative that flows into the other.

Good storytelling takes practice. Keep at it!

### Passage:

John had always been a restless sleeper, often waking up in different parts of his house, with no recollection of how he got there. It was a quirky trait that his family found amusing but never took seriously. What they didn’t know was that John had unknowingly taken up sleepwalking as a nighttime hobby. During the day, John was an average person, but at night, he would embark on nocturnal adventures that he would never remember.

One of his favorite spots to venture into was the abandoned overpass near his house. The overpass went under the highway and into a secret world. It was a place where the outside world seemed to fade away, filled with the echoes of a past life that had long since vanished. The sound of distant traffic above was soothing, and the cool, damp air kept him focused.

In this hidden world, John would often pick up a brush and paint the walls of the abandoned overpass as though he were trying to give it a second chance at life. However, his art never progressed when he literally used his sweat and tears. The paint would sometimes run, blending into each other creating a mess that confused him. He would spend hours lost in his thoughts, adding layers of paint that reflected his emotional state, not just the environment around him.

Driving with John was always a bit of an adventure since he loved the highway. He insisted that certain parts of the road be treated as a game, viewing the safety cones as a slalom course. Driving with him often involved shouting and warning him about the true dangers of the road. There is one other thing that has always been a secret fear to his friends, it was always dangerous to drive with him especially when you saw the tears running down his eyes. John would always have an air of seriousness when he drove, no one dared to interrupt his concentration as they knew he would lose it.

In the quiet suburban town of Meadowgrove, Jake lived a seemingly ordinary life. He worked a mundane job at the local hardware store and spent his evenings engaging in various hobbies. However, his nights were a different story. He had unknowingly taken up sleepwalking as a nighttime hobby. The overpass, which every night would find him driving over,  was an intriguing part of his daily commute, and he would always find himself passing by it.

It wasn't until one particularly restless night that Jake stumbled upon a fascinating secret. The overpass went under the highway and into a secret world. No one knew where the exit would lead to, but it had led him to some places he could never have imagined. The eerie silence and the mystery of it all captivated him, making him want to discover more each time.

During the day, Jake also pursued his passion for painting. He would spend countless hours in his small studio, dipping his brushes into the vivid colors and bringing his visions to life on canvas. However he found his art never progressed when he literally used his sweat and tears. It was a labor of love, but he also found it frustrating at times, especially when he was constantly finding paint dried in his hair, and brushes in unusual places.

Driving with Jake was always an adventure, but it was always dangerous to drive with him since he insisted the safety cones were a slalom course. His erratic driving habits often led to tense moments, as he would suddenly swerve to avoid imaginary obstacles or hit the brakes unexpectedly. Despite the warnings from his friends and family, Jake remained unfazed, continuing to approach driving with the same playful abandon he applied to his sleepwalking escapades and his art.

Jim had always wanted to channel his emotions through art. He tried painting, sculpture, even pottery, but his creations never seemed to capture the depth of his feelings. Over time he felt trapped in the process, his art never progressed when he literally used his sweat and tears.

He insisted on making each drive an adventure, especially in busy parking lots lined with vividly colored plastic cones. He saw them as a slalom course he insisted on navigating instead of a safety measure. It was always dangerous to drive with him since he insisted the safety cones were a slalom course.
